note: Each cluster needs a γ verdict. See GAMMA_SYSTEM_PROMPT in src/deck_builder/gamma_llm.py for the rules.

senses.text: safe and well; in a calm or happy state
examples: Are you OK?

senses.text: all right; acceptable; in an acceptable way
examples: Is it OK if I leave now?
Is it OK for me to come too?
Does my hair look okay?
I think I did OK in the exam.
Whatever you decide, it's okay by me.

senses.text: the deliberate ending of a pregnancy at an early stage
examples: to support/oppose abortion
a woman’s right to abortion
the country's strict abortion laws
Both pro-choice and anti-abortion groups want to ensure their voices are heard.

senses.text: a medical operation to end a pregnancy at an early stage
examples: She decided to have an abortion.
Some medical staff wanted the right to refuse to perform abortions.

senses.text: not in a place because of illness, etc.
examples: She was absent from work for two weeks.
Local people were conspicuously absent from the meeting.

senses.text: not present in something
examples: Love was totally absent from his childhood.
She now played with a passion that had been strangely absent from her previous performance.

senses.text: to take in a liquid, gas or other substance from the surface or space around
examples: Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air.
Let the rice cook until it has absorbed all the water.
The cream is easily absorbed into the skin.

senses.text: to take in and keep heat, light, energy, sound, etc. instead of reflecting it
examples: Black walls absorb a lot of heat during the day.

senses.text: based on general ideas and not on any particular real person, thing or situation
examples: abstract knowledge/principles
The research shows that pre-school children are capable of thinking in abstract terms.

senses.text: existing in thought or as an idea but not having a physical reality
examples: We may talk of beautiful things but beauty itself is abstract.

senses.text: not representing people or things in a realistic way, but expressing the artist’s ideas about them
examples: the work of American abstract expressionists like Mark Rothko

senses.text: the opportunity or right to use something or to see somebody/something
examples: High-speed internet access has become a necessity.
You need a password to get access to the computer system.
access to information/services/resources
access to healthcare/education
They are seeking increased access to markets overseas.

senses.text: a way of entering or reaching a place
examples: The police gained access through a broken window.
Many paths have become overgrown, blocking access.
There is easy access by road.
We welcome disabled visitors and there is wheelchair access to all parts of the park.
The only access to the farmhouse is across the fields.

senses.text: a place to live, work or stay in
examples: rented/temporary accommodation
Hotel accommodation is included in the price of your holiday.
We may have to provide alternative accommodation for you.
First-class accommodation is available on all flights.
It can be difficult to find suitable accommodation for those in need.

senses.text: somewhere to live or stay, often also providing food or other services
examples: More and more travelers are looking for bed and breakfast accommodations in private homes.
The boat provides reasonable overnight accommodations for four adults.

senses.text: to gradually get more and more of something over a period of time
examples: I seem to have accumulated a lot of books.
By investing wisely she accumulated a fortune.
We have accumulated a great amount of evidence.

senses.text: to gradually increase in number or quantity over a period of time
examples: Debts began to accumulate.
Dust and dirt soon accumulate if a house is not cleaned regularly.

senses.text: to accept that something is true
examples: She refuses to acknowledge the need for reform.
I have to acknowledge the fact that I am partly responsible.
It's a generally acknowledged fact.
Most people acknowledge the existence of evil.
to openly/publicly/readily acknowledge something

senses.text: to accept that somebody/something has a particular authority or status
examples: The country acknowledged his claim to the throne.
The peace settlement effectively acknowledged the country's independence.
He is widely acknowledged as the best player in the world.
He is widely acknowledged to be the best player in the world.

senses.text: the act of getting something, especially knowledge, a skill, etc.
examples: theories of child language acquisition

senses.text: something that somebody buys to add to what they already own, usually something valuable
examples: His latest acquisition is a racehorse.
The money will be spent on acquisitions for the university library.

senses.text: a company, piece of land, etc. bought by somebody, especially another company; the act of buying it
examples: They have made acquisitions in several EU countries.
the acquisition of shares by employees
The group has announced its first overseas acquisition: a successful software company.
The company has just made another acquisition.

senses.text: the process of doing something in order to make something happen or to deal with a situation
examples: The time has come for action if these beautiful animals are to survive.
to threaten/demand/call for action
Firefighters took action immediately to stop the blaze spreading.
Divers were close at hand, ready to go into action if the stunt went wrong.
What is the best course of action in the circumstances?

senses.text: a thing that somebody does
examples: Each of us must take responsibility for our own actions.
They sought to justify their actions.

senses.text: to agree, often unwillingly, that something is true
examples: It was a stupid thing to do, I admit.
‘I'm very nervous,’ she admitted reluctantly.
Don't be afraid to admit to your mistakes.
She admits to being strict with her children.
He admitted all his mistakes.

senses.text: to say that you have done something wrong or illegal
examples: He refused to admit to the other charges.
She admitted to having stolen the car.
She admitted theft.
He refused to admit his guilt.
The hospital has admitted liability for the accident.

senses.text: a thing that helps you to be better or more successful than other people
examples: a distinct/significant/huge advantage
It gives you an unfair advantage (= something that benefits you, but not your opponents).
She had the advantage of a good education.
You will be at an advantage (= have an advantage) in the interview if you have thought about the questions in advance.
The company was able to gain a competitive advantage over its rivals by reducing costs.

senses.text: a quality of something that makes it better or more useful
examples: Each of these systems has its advantages and disadvantages.
Continuous assessment offers clear advantages for teachers and students.
A small car has the added advantage of being cheaper to run.
Is there any advantage in getting there early?
There are many advantages to online shopping.

senses.text: an unusual, exciting or dangerous experience, journey or series of events
examples: her adventures travelling in Africa
adventure stories
Four members of our staff have embarked on the adventure of a lifetime.
They fly around in their rocket ship and go on exciting adventures.
When you're a child, life is one big adventure.

senses.text: the quality of being excited and willing to take risks, try new ideas, etc.
examples: a sense/spirit of adventure
I set out across the country looking for adventure.
the adventure travel industry
Travelling by train has always been associated with romance and adventure.

senses.text: to tell the public about a product or a service in order to encourage people to buy or to use it
examples: If you want to attract customers you need to advertise.
to advertise on TV/online/on social media
to advertise a product/service
a glossy brochure advertising holidays in the Maldives
How to Advertise a Business for Free on the Internet

senses.text: to let people know that something is going to happen, or that a job is available by giving details about it in a newspaper, on a notice in a public place, on the internet, etc.
examples: Sellers pay upfront to advertise.
We are currently advertising for a new sales manager.
The best jobs are not always advertised in newspapers.
Vacancies are usually advertised on our website.
We advertised the concert quite widely.

senses.text: to tell somebody what you think they should do in a particular situation
examples: I would strongly advise against going out on your own.
They advised me against visiting the troubled south of the country.
The Foreign Office is advising Britons against all non-essential travel to the region.
They advised against the use of military force.
Her mother was away and couldn't advise her.

senses.text: to give somebody help and information on a subject that you know a lot about
examples: We employ an expert to advise on new technology.
The nurse will advise about getting out of bed, bathing, and diet.
She advises the government on environmental issues.
They can advise customers about safely using and storing pesticides.
The pharmacist will advise which medicines are safe to take.

senses.text: feeling fear; frightened because you think that you might be hurt or suffer
examples: Don't be afraid.
It's all over. There's nothing to be afraid of now.
He had always been afraid of dogs.
They know I'm afraid of heights.
Are you afraid of spiders?

senses.text: worried about what might happen
examples: She was afraid of upsetting her parents.
He was afraid of making a fool of himself.
Don't be afraid to ask if you don't understand.
She certainly isn't afraid to speak her mind.
We were afraid (that) we were going to capsize the boat.

senses.text: worried or frightened that something unpleasant, dangerous, etc. will happen to a particular person or thing
examples: I'm not afraid for me, but for the baby.
They had already fired three people and he was afraid for his job.
I am very afraid for their safety.
I was afraid for my life.

senses.text: a business or an organization that provides a particular service especially when representing other businesses or organizations
examples: She works for an advertising agency.
He managed to find a job through an agency.

senses.text: a government department that provides a particular service
examples: law enforcement agencies
Some laboratories are operated by government agencies.

senses.text: feelings of anger and hate that may result in threatening or violent behaviour
examples: Video games have been blamed for causing aggression.
Nowadays our aggression is channelled into sports.

senses.text: a violent attack or threats by one person against another person or by one country against another country
examples: unprovoked military aggression

senses.text: angry, and behaving in a threatening way; ready to attack
examples: Seals have been known to exhibit aggressive behaviour towards swimmers.
They became increasingly aggressive as the evening wore on.
There's no need to get aggressive.
She warned that her dog was aggressive towards strangers.

senses.text: determined and acting with force in order to succeed
examples: a very aggressive advertising campaign
We need to get more aggressive in our approach.
The firm is extremely aggressive in seeking new markets.

senses.text: drinks such as beer, wine, etc. that can make people drunk
examples: He never drinks alcohol.
alcohol abuse/use/dependence
the dangers of excessive alcohol consumption
Alcohol should only be consumed in moderation.
Licences to sell alcohol are granted by local councils.

senses.text: the clear liquid that is found in drinks such as beer, wine, etc. and is used in medicines, cleaning products, etc.
examples: Wine contains about 10% alcohol.
The food and drink industry has responded to the demand for low- and no-alcohol drinks.
levels of alcohol in the blood
She was arrested for driving under the influence of alcohol.
He pleaded guilty to driving with excess alcohol.

senses.text: able to think quickly; quick to notice things
examples: Suddenly he found himself awake and fully alert.
Two alert scientists spotted the mistake.
Try to stay alert while driving at night.
an alert mind
mentally alert

senses.text: aware of something, especially a problem or danger
examples: We must be alert to the possibility of danger.
She was alert to the slightest sound.

senses.text: an agreement between countries, political parties, etc. to work together in order to achieve something that they all want
examples: to form/make an alliance
The Social Democrats are now in alliance with the Greens.
an alliance between education and business to develop the use of technology in schools

senses.text: a group of people, political parties, etc. who work together in order to achieve something that they all want
examples: The Green Alliance was formed to campaign against environmental damage.

senses.text: without any other people
examples: Tom is not alone in finding Rick hard to work with.
She waited until she was alone with him to speak.
He was afraid of being alone with his thoughts.
He lives alone.
She was sitting all alone in the hall.

senses.text: without the help of other people or things
examples: It's hard bringing up children alone.
The assassin said he had acted alone.

senses.text: that can be understood in more than one way; having different meanings
examples: an ambiguous word/term/statement
Her account was deliberately ambiguous.

senses.text: not clearly stated or defined
examples: His role has always been ambiguous.

senses.text: a comparison of one thing with another thing that has similar features; a feature that is similar
examples: The teacher drew an analogy between the human heart and a pump.
There are no analogies with any previous legal cases.

senses.text: the process of comparing one thing with another thing that has similar features in order to explain it
examples: learning by analogy
We can understand this theory by analogy with human beings.

senses.text: the detailed study or examination of something in order to understand more about it; the result of the study
examples: statistical/data analysis
a detailed analysis of the data
to perform/conduct an analysis
Further analysis revealed significant regional variations in the results.
We made the decision based on our analysis of the situation.

senses.text: a careful examination of a substance in order to find out what it consists of
examples: The blood samples are sent to the laboratory for analysis.
the results of a DNA sequence analysis
You can ask for a chemical analysis of your tap water.

senses.text: a set of rooms for living in, usually on one floor of a building
examples: an apartment building
I was renting a two-bedroom apartment in an old building.
A group of developers wants to build a high-rise apartment complex.
I lived on the ground floor of an apartment block.
They left their fourth-floor apartment on Mott Street and walked to the bus.

senses.text: a set of rooms used for a holiday
examples: self-catering holiday apartments
They are staying in a luxury apartment overlooking the beach.

senses.text: the tools or other pieces of equipment that are needed for a particular activity or task
examples: a piece of laboratory apparatus
Firefighters needed breathing apparatus to enter the burning house.

senses.text: the structure of a system or an organization, particularly that of a political party or a government
examples: the power of the state apparatus
We cannot do without the apparatus of government in protecting the rights of the individual.

senses.text: to start to be seen
examples: Three days later a rash appeared.
Symptoms usually start appearing within two to three days.
A cat suddenly appeared out of nowhere.

A bus appeared around the corner.

senses.text: to begin to exist or be known or used for the first time
examples: Scientists are unsure when the virus first appeared.
His work began to appear on the scene in the 1960s.
When did mammals appear on the earth?

senses.text: the fact of somebody/something arriving, especially when it is not expected
examples: The sudden appearance of a security guard caused them to drop the money and run.
I don't want to go to the party, but I suppose I'd better put in an appearance (= go there for a short time).

senses.text: the moment at which something begins to exist or starts to be seen or used
examples: the early appearance of daffodils in spring
the appearance of organic vegetables in the supermarkets

senses.text: the feeling that somebody/something is good or acceptable; a positive opinion of somebody/something
examples: She desperately wanted to win her parents' approval.
Do the plans meet with your approval?

senses.text: agreement to, or permission for something, especially a plan or request
examples: The plan will be submitted to the committee for official approval.
parliamentary/congressional/government approval

I can't agree to anything without my partner's approval.
planning approvals

senses.text: to happen; to start to exist
examples: An opportunity arose to work in the United States.

senses.text: to happen as a result of a particular situation
examples: injuries arising out of a road accident

senses.text: a large organized group of soldiers who are trained to fight on land
examples: The two opposing armies faced each other across the battlefield.

senses.text: the part of a country’s armed forces that fights on land
examples: Her sister is in the army.
After leaving school, Mike went into the army.
He joined the army at the age of 16.
an army officer/chief
army barracks/bases

senses.text: the use of the imagination to express ideas or feelings, particularly in painting, drawing or sculpture
examples: modern/contemporary art
an art critic/historian/lover
Can we call television art?
stolen works of art

senses.text: examples of objects such as paintings, drawings or sculptures
examples: an art gallery/exhibition
a collection of art and antiques

senses.text: the skill of creating objects such as paintings and drawings, especially when you study it
examples: She's good at art and design.

an art teacher/student/college/class

senses.text: made or produced to copy something natural; not real
examples: an artificial limb/flower/sweetener/fertilizer
artificial lighting/light

senses.text: created by people; not happening naturally
examples: A job interview is a very artificial situation.
the artificial barriers of race, class and gender

senses.text: not what it appears to be
examples: artificial emotion

senses.text: the grey or black powder that is left after something, especially tobacco, wood or coal, has burnt
examples: cigarette ash
black volcanic ash

senses.text: what is left after something has been destroyed by burning
examples: The town was reduced to ashes in the fighting.
the glowing ashes of the campfire

senses.text: to come together as a group; to bring people or things together as a group

senses.text: to fit together all the separate parts of something, for example a piece of furniture

senses.text: a person who helps or supports somebody, usually in their job
examples: My assistant will now demonstrate the machine in action.

senses.text: a person whose job is to serve customers in a shop

senses.text: a person who competes in sports

senses.text: a person who competes in sports such as running, jumping and throwing
examples: He played baseball and basketball and excelled as a track athlete.

senses.text: an act of trying to do something, especially something difficult, often with no success

senses.text: an act of trying to kill somebody

an assassination attempt

senses.text: to be present at an event

senses.text: to go regularly to a place

senses.text: the act of being present at a place, for example at school

senses.text: the number of people present at an organized event
examples: high/low/falling/poor attendances

senses.text: pleasant to look at, especially in a sexual way

I like John but I don't find him attractive physically.

senses.text: pleasant
examples: a big house with an attractive garden
Your new glasses are very attractive.

That's one of the less attractive aspects of her personality.

senses.text: the group of people who have gathered to watch or listen to something (a play, concert, somebody speaking, etc.)

senses.text: a number of people or a particular group of people who watch, read, or listen to the same thing

senses.text: the power to give orders to people

senses.text: the power or right to do something

senses.text: the freedom for a country, a region or an organization to govern itself independently

senses.text: the ability to act and make decisions without being controlled by anyone else
examples: giving individuals greater autonomy in their own lives

senses.text: everything that can be seen around an event or scene

senses.text: the general conditions in which an event takes place, which sometimes help to explain that event

senses.text: a painted piece of cloth that is hung behind the stage in a theatre as part of the scenery

senses.text: a container made of cloth, leather, plastic or paper, used to carry things in, especially when shopping or travelling
examples: He was carrying a heavy bag of groceries.

a make-up bag
a baby changing bag

senses.text: a bag or case that you take when you are travelling; a piece of luggage
examples: I packed my bags and left for the airport.
He's upstairs unpacking his bags.

senses.text: a style of dancing that tells a dramatic story with music but no talking or singing
examples: She wants to be a ballet dancer.
ballet shoes

senses.text: a story or work of art performed by a group of ballet dancers
examples: ‘Swan Lake’ is one of the great classical ballets.

senses.text: to decide or say officially that something is not allowed
examples: This law bans the use of dangerous pesticides.

senses.text: to order somebody not to do something, go somewhere, etc., especially officially

senses.text: a place where you can buy and drink alcoholic and other drinks

senses.text: a place in which a particular kind of food or drink is the main thing that is served
examples: a sushi bar

senses.text: a large round container, usually made of wood or metal, with flat ends and, usually, curved sides
examples: a beer/wine barrel

senses.text: the contents of or the amount contained in a barrel; a unit of measurement in the oil industry equal to between 120 and 159 litres

senses.text: a room in which there is a bath, a washbasin and often a toilet

senses.text: a room in which there is a toilet, a sink and sometimes a bath or shower
examples: I have to go to the bathroom (= use the toilet).

senses.text: an alcoholic drink made from malt with hops added to give it taste. There are many types of beer.
examples: a pint/can/bottle of beer
a barrel/glass of beer
beers brewed in Germany
a beer glass/bottle/can

senses.text: a glass, bottle or can of beer
examples: Shall we have a beer?

senses.text: to do things in a particular way
examples: The doctor behaved very unprofessionally.

senses.text: to do things in a way that people think is correct or polite
examples: Will you kids just behave!

senses.text: the way that somebody behaves, especially towards other people

social/sexual/criminal behaviour

senses.text: the way a person, an animal, a plant, a chemical, etc. behaves or functions in a particular situation
examples: studying human and animal behaviour

senses.text: a strong feeling that something/somebody exists or is true; confidence that something/somebody is good or right
examples: I admire his passionate belief in what he is doing.

senses.text: an opinion about something; something that you think is true

senses.text: something that you believe, especially as part of your religion

senses.text: loved very much

senses.text: loved very much by somebody; very popular with somebody
examples: the deep purple flowers so beloved by artists

senses.text: a long seat for two or more people, usually made of wood
examples: a park bench

senses.text: the seats where players sit when they are not playing in the game
examples: the substitutes’ bench

senses.text: of the most excellent type or quality
examples: That's the best movie I've ever seen!

Is that your best suit?

senses.text: giving most pleasure; happiest

senses.text: most suitable or appropriate

senses.text: of a higher standard or less poor quality; not as bad as something else
examples: We're hoping for better weather tomorrow.

senses.text: more suitable or appropriate

She nodded towards his suitcase. ‘Going somewhere?’ she asked.

senses.text: making a lot of noise
examples: noisy children/traffic/crowds
He was kept awake by noisy neighbours.
a noisy protest (= when people shout)
The kids were even noisier than the dogs.
The engine is very noisy at high speed.

senses.text: full of noise
examples: a noisy classroom/office
People find it difficult to concentrate in noisy environments such as open-plan offices.
The streets were very noisy throughout the night.
The party was getting a bit noisy.
It was so noisy I couldn't hear myself speak.

senses.text: to formally suggest that somebody/something should be chosen for an important role, prize, position, etc.
examples: She has been nominated for the presidency.
He was nominated (as) best actor.
I nominated Paul to take on the role of treasurer.

senses.text: to choose somebody to do a particular job
examples: I have been nominated to the committee.
The president wanted to nominate her as his successor.
She was nominated to speak on our behalf.
Each office will nominate a representative to sit on the committee.

senses.text: ideas, statements or beliefs that you think are silly or not true
examples: Reports that he has resigned are nonsense.
You're talking nonsense!
‘I won't go.’ ‘Nonsense! You must go!’
It's nonsense to say they don't care.
The idea is an economic nonsense.

senses.text: spoken or written words that have no meaning or make no sense
examples: a book of children’s nonsense poems
Most of the translation he did for me was complete nonsense.
The whole article seemed like complete nonsense to me.

senses.text: the direction that is on your left when you watch the sun rise; one of the four main points of the compass
examples: Which way is north?
cold winds coming from the north
a north-facing wall
Mount Kenya is to the north of (= further north than) Nairobi.
views of distant mountains to north and south

senses.text: the northern part of a country, a region or the world
examples: birds migrating from the north
Houses are less expensive in the North (= of England) than in the South.
They wanted to escape the cold of the north.
The main road ran across the north of the island.
There's been a severe earthquake in the north of the country.

senses.text: a small book of plain paper for writing notes in
examples: The police officer wrote the details down in his notebook.
She kept a notebook (= wrote notes in it) during the trip.

senses.text: a small book for students to write their work in
a sheet of paper giving written or printed information, usually put in a public place
examples: There was a notice on the board saying the class had been cancelled.
A public notice about the planned development was pinned to the wall.

senses.text: a board or sign giving information, an instruction or a warning
examples: There was a notice saying ‘Keep off the Grass’.
There are notices about where to park.

senses.text: to see or hear somebody/something; to become aware of somebody/something
examples: People were making fun of him but he didn't seem to notice.
The first thing I noticed about the room was the smell.
I began to notice something strange.
You can't fail to notice the poverty of the region.
to barely/hardly/scarcely notice something

senses.text: to pay attention to somebody/something
examples: Notice the way the bridge is supported by its suspension cables.

senses.text: the state of being forced to do something because it is your duty, or because of a law, etc.
examples: You are under no obligation to buy anything.
She did not feel under any obligation to tell him the truth.
I don't want people coming to see me out of a sense of obligation.
We will send you an estimate for the work without obligation (= you do not have to accept it).

senses.text: something which you must do because you have promised, because of a law, etc.
examples: legal/professional/financial obligations
They reminded him of his contractual obligations.
The country has promised to comply with its international obligations.
We have a moral obligation to protect the environment.
I have an obligation under law to protect their confidentiality.

senses.text: not well known
examples: an obscure German poet
We went to see one of Shakespeare’s more obscure plays.
He was born around 1650 but his origins remain obscure.
The origins of the tradition have become obscure.

senses.text: difficult to understand
examples: I found her lecture very obscure.
For some obscure reason, he failed to turn up.
The meaning of his comment was obscure to everyone but himself.

senses.text: to see or notice somebody/something
examples: Have you observed any changes lately?
All the characters in the novel are closely observed (= seem like people in real life).
I want you to observe all the details.
The patterns observed in our study may represent a general rule.
The police observed a man enter the bank.

senses.text: to watch somebody/something carefully, especially to learn more about them
examples: He observes keenly, but says little.
I felt he was observing everything I did.
The patients were observed over a period of several months.
an observed decrease in rainfall in the area
The program provided an opportunity to observe the effects of treatment.

senses.text: the state in which a person’s mind is completely filled with thoughts of one particular thing or person in a way that is not reasonable or normal
examples: Her fear of flying is bordering on obsession.
There's a national obsession with celebrity in England.
The media's obsession with the young prince continues.

senses.text: a person or thing that somebody thinks about too much
examples: Fitness has become an obsession with him.

senses.text: something that happens or exists
examples: a common/everyday/frequent/regular occurrence
Vandalism used to be a rare occurrence here.
The program counts the number of occurrences of any word within the text.

senses.text: the fact of something happening or existing
examples: the recent occurrence of skin cancer in younger patients

senses.text: the mass of salt water that covers most of the earth’s surface
examples: The ship was dredged from the depths of the ocean.
People were swimming in the ocean despite the hurricane warning.
The plane hit the ocean several miles offshore.
Our beach house is just a couple of miles from the ocean.
ships large enough to sail the open ocean

senses.text: one of the five large areas that the ocean is divided into
examples: the Antarctic/Arctic/Atlantic/Indian/Pacific Ocean
They travelled across oceans in search of a new life.

senses.text: the degree to which something is likely to happen
examples: The odds are very much in our favour (= we are likely to succeed).
The odds are heavily against him (= he is not likely to succeed).
The odds are that (= it is likely that) she'll win.
What are the odds (= how likely is it) he won't turn up?

senses.text: something that makes it seem impossible to do or achieve something
examples: They secured a victory in the face of overwhelming odds.
She defied the odds to beat the clear favourite.
to beat/overcome the odds
The film is a heart-warming tale of triumph against the odds.
Against all (the) odds, he made a full recovery.

senses.text: the connection between two numbers that shows how much money somebody will receive if they win a bet
examples: odds of ten to one (= ten times the amount of money that has been bet by somebody will be paid to them if they win)
They are offering long/short odds (= the prize money will be high/low because there is a high/low risk of losing) on the defending champion.
I'll lay odds on him getting the job (= I'm sure he will get it).

senses.text: a room, set of rooms or building where people work, usually sitting at desks
examples: The company is moving to new offices on the other side of town.
Are you going to the office today?
I'm sorry, Mr Anders is not in the office today.
I've had a tough day at the office.
a regional/branch office

senses.text: a room in which a particular person works, usually at a desk
examples: Some people have to share an office.
Come into my office.

senses.text: a room or building used for a particular purpose, especially to provide information or a service
examples: a ticket office

senses.text: not modern; no longer fashionable
examples: old-fashioned clothes/styles/methods/equipment
These sweets are still made in the old-fashioned way.
The Winnedumah Hotel has old-fashioned charm.
His clothes were curiously old-fashioned.
Critics regarded her films as hopelessly old-fashioned.

senses.text: believing in old or traditional ways; having traditional ideas
examples: My parents are old-fashioned about relationships and marriage.
She wants to be an old-fashioned mother, staying at home and looking after the children.
Call me old-fashioned, but I still believe in good manners.
She's somewhat old-fashioned in her attitudes.
He seemed too old-fashioned and formal.

senses.text: connected with the way in which a business, machine, system, etc. works
examples: operational activities/costs/difficulties

senses.text: ready or available to be used
examples: The new airport is now fully operational.

senses.text: something that you can choose to have or do; the freedom to choose what you do
examples: As I see it, we have two options…
There are various options open to you.
to explore/consider/look at all your options
We are currently studying all the options available.
Going to college was not an option for me.

senses.text: a subject that a student can choose to study, but that they do not have to do
examples: The course offers options in design and computing.

senses.text: one of the choices you can make when using a computer program
examples: Choose the ‘Cut’ option from the Edit menu.
Look at the on-screen menu and select the ‘File’ option.

senses.text: not unusual or different in any way
examples: an ordinary sort of day
in the ordinary course of events
ordinary people like you and me
This was no ordinary meeting.
She was a perfectly ordinary little girl.

senses.text: having no unusual or interesting features
examples: The meal was very ordinary.

senses.text: the act of making arrangements or preparations for something
examples: I leave most of the organization of these conferences to my assistant.
A wedding on that scale takes a lot of organization.

senses.text: the way in which the different parts of something are arranged
examples: The report studies the organization of labour within the company.
They experimented with new forms of social organization.

senses.text: the quality of being arranged in a neat, careful and logical way
examples: She is highly intelligent but her work lacks organization.
The website would be more effective with better organization.

senses.text: to make all the arrangements for something to happen or be provided
examples: He helped to organize various events.
to organize a conference/meeting
to organize a protest/demonstration/rally
to organize a workshop/seminar
I'll invite people if you can organize food and drinks.

senses.text: to arrange something or the parts of something into a particular order or structure
examples: Modern computers can organize large amounts of data very quickly.
You should try and organize your time better.
We do not fully understand how the brain is organized.
The complex is organized around a central courtyard.
We need to organize the work according to the availability and skills of each volunteer.

senses.text: involving large numbers of people who work together to do something in a way that has been carefully planned
examples: an organized body of workers
an organized system of childcare

39% of male respondents stated that they regularly participated in organized sport compared with 17% of female respondents.

senses.text: arranged or planned well or in the way mentioned
examples: a carefully organized campaign
a well-organized office
a badly organized event
a loosely organized political alliance

senses.text: able to plan your work, life, etc. well and in an efficient way
examples: a very organized person
Isn't it time you started to get organized?

senses.text: a shop or an organization that sells goods made by a particular company or of a particular type
examples: The business has 34 retail outlets in this state alone.

senses.text: a shop that sells goods of a particular make at reduced prices
examples: the Nike outlet in the outlet mall
Here you'll find factory outlets that sell to the public directly.

senses.text: the amount of something that a person, a machine or an organization produces
examples: Manufacturing output has increased by 8 per cent.

senses.text: the information, results, etc. produced by a computer
examples: data output
an output device

senses.text: to put clothes, etc. into a bag in preparation for a trip away from home
examples: I haven't packed yet.
I have to start packing for my trip.
I haven't packed my suitcase yet.
I packed my bags and left.
Did you pack the camera?

senses.text: to put something into a container so that it can be stored, transported or sold
examples: The pottery was packed in boxes and shipped to the US.
I carefully packed up the gifts.
Ryan busied himself packing everything up into bags.
He found a part-time job packing eggs.

senses.text: something that is wrapped in paper or put into a thick envelope so that it can be sent by mail, carried easily, or given as a present
examples: A large package has arrived for you.

senses.text: a box, bag, etc. in which things are wrapped or packed; the contents of a box, etc.
examples: Check the list of ingredients on the side of the package.
a package of hamburger buns

senses.text: a small container made of paper or card in which goods are packed for selling
examples: a packet of biscuits/cigarettes/crisps

senses.text: a small object wrapped in paper or put into a thick envelope so that it can be sent by mail, carried easily or given as a present
examples: A packet of photographs arrived with the mail.

senses.text: a closed plastic or paper package that contains a very small amount of liquid or a powder
examples: a packet of instant cocoa mix

senses.text: causing you pain
examples: Is your back still painful?
a painful death
My ankle is still too painful to walk on.

It was a difficult and painful operation.

senses.text: causing you to feel upset or embarrassed
examples: Seeing her again brought back painful memories.
My parents' divorce was a very painful experience for me.
Christmas can be a painful reminder of the loss of a loved one.
Their efforts were painful to watch.
I find it extremely painful to read the daily news.

senses.text: to cover a surface or object with paint
examples: Paint the shed with weather-resistant paint.
a brightly painted barge
a freshly painted fence
We've had the house painted.
The walls were painted yellow.

senses.text: to make a picture or design using paints
examples: to paint portraits/pictures
A friend painted the children for me (= painted a picture of the children).
Slogans had been painted on the walls.
She paints in oils.
a portrait painted in oils

senses.text: the official home of a king, queen, president, etc.
examples: Buckingham Palace
the royal/presidential palace

the palace gates/grounds/walls

senses.text: any large impressive house
examples: The Old Town has a whole collection of churches, palaces and mosques.

senses.text: a sudden feeling of great fear that cannot be controlled and prevents you from thinking clearly
examples: a moment of panic

senses.text: a situation in which people are made to feel very anxious or frightened, causing them to act quickly and without thinking carefully
examples: News of the losses caused (a) panic among investors.

There's no panic (= we do not need to rush), we've got plenty of time.
panic buying/selling (= the act of buying/selling things quickly and without thinking carefully because you are afraid that a particular situation will become worse)

senses.text: a public celebration of a special day or event, usually with bands in the streets and decorated vehicles
examples: the Lord Mayor’s parade

senses.text: a formal occasion when soldiers march or stand in lines so that they can be inspected (= looked at and approved) by their officers or other important people
examples: a military parade

senses.text: a person, a situation, an event, etc. that is very similar to another, especially one in a different place or time
examples: These ideas have parallels in Freud's thought too.
This tradition has no parallel in our culture.

senses.text: similar features
examples: There are interesting parallels between the 1960s and the late 1990s.

senses.text: an area of land used for a particular purpose
examples: a business/science park
a wildlife park
a park ranger

senses.text: a piece of land for playing sports, especially baseball
examples: With a mighty swing he hit the ball right out of the park.

senses.text: the act of stopping a vehicle at a place and leaving it there for a period of time
examples: There is no parking here between 9 a.m. and 6 p.m.

senses.text: a space or an area for leaving vehicles
examples: The hotel is centrally situated with ample free parking.
parking for 300 cars

senses.text: the group of people who are elected to make and change the laws of a country
examples: The German parliament is called the ‘Bundestag’.

senses.text: the parliament of some countries, including the United Kingdom. In the UK, Parliament consists of the House of Commons and the House of Lords
examples: a Member of Parliament
an Act of Parliament

to win a seat in Parliament
The party is set to win a majority in Parliament (= the most votes) in October's election.

senses.text: a series of letters, numbers, etc. that you must type into a computer or computer system in order to be able to use it
examples: Enter a username and password to get into the system.

senses.text: a secret word or phrase that you need to know in order to be allowed into a place
examples: You must give the password before they'll let you in.

senses.text: the ability to stay calm and accept a delay or something annoying without complaining
examples: She has little patience with (= will not accept or consider) such views.

senses.text: the ability to spend a lot of time doing something difficult that needs a lot of attention and effort

senses.text: a person who is receiving medical treatment, especially in a hospital

senses.text: a person who receives treatment from a particular doctor, dentist, etc.
examples: He's one of Dr Shaw's patients.

senses.text: the act of going to different parts of a building, an area, etc. to make sure that there is no trouble or crime
examples: Security guards make regular patrols at night.
a police car on patrol

senses.text: a group of soldiers, vehicles, etc. that patrol an area
examples: a naval/police patrol
a patrol car/boat

senses.text: to give somebody money for work, goods, services, etc.
examples: Who's paying?
I'll pay for the tickets.

senses.text: to give somebody money that you owe them
examples: to pay a fee/bill/fine/debt

Membership fees should be paid to the secretary.

senses.text: the way you notice things, especially with the senses
examples: Everyone's perception of reality is slightly different.
visual perception

senses.text: the ability to understand the true nature of something
examples: She showed great perception in her assessment of the family situation.

senses.text: having everything that is necessary; complete and without faults or weaknesses

senses.text: completely correct; exact and accurate
examples: She speaks perfect English.

senses.text: the best of its kind

senses.text: excellent; very good
examples: The weather was perfect.

senses.text: exactly right for somebody/something
examples: Hawaii is the perfect place for a honeymoon.

senses.text: a particular length of time
examples: a long/an extended period
a short/brief period
a period of transition/uncertainty/expansion
a period of two years/six months/four weeks
a two-year/six-month/four-week period

senses.text: a length of time in the life of a particular person or in the history of a particular country
examples: Which period of history would you most like to have lived in?

senses.text: to allow somebody to do something or to allow something to happen
examples: Mobile phones are not permitted in the examination room.

senses.text: to make something possible
examples: We hope to visit the cathedral, if time permits.

senses.text: determined to do something despite difficulties, especially when other people are against you and think that you are being annoying or unreasonable

senses.text: continuing for a long period of time, or repeated frequently, especially in a way that is annoying and cannot be stopped

senses.text: a human as an individual
examples: What sort of person would do a thing like that?
He's a fascinating person.

senses.text: a human, especially one who is not identified
examples: A certain person (= somebody that I do not wish to name) told me about it.

senses.text: your own; not belonging to or connected with anyone else
examples: The novel is written from personal experience.

senses.text: between individual people who know each other
examples: Having good personal relationships is the most important thing for me.

senses.text: to make somebody do something by giving them good reasons for doing it
examples: Try to persuade him to come.

senses.text: to make somebody believe that something is true

senses.text: a group of words that have a particular meaning when used together

senses.text: a small group of words (usually without a finite verb) that together have a particular meaning and that typically form part of a sentence. ‘the green car’ and ‘on Friday morning’ are phrases.
examples: a two-word phrase

senses.text: a number of things that have been placed on top of each other
examples: a pile of clothes/paper

senses.text: a mass of something that is high in the middle and wider at the bottom than at the top

senses.text: a lot of something

senses.text: to put things one on top of another; to form a pile
examples: She piled the boxes one on top of the other.

senses.text: to put something on/into something; to load something with something
examples: The sofa was piled high with cushions.

senses.text: a disease spread by rats that causes a high temperature, swellings (= areas that are larger and rounder than usual) on the body and usually death
examples: a decline in population following outbreaks of plague

senses.text: any disease that spreads quickly and kills a lot of people

senses.text: something that you intend to do or achieve
examples: Do you have any plans for the summer?

senses.text: a set of things to do in order to achieve something, especially one that has been considered in detail in advance

senses.text: a detailed drawing of a machine, building, etc. that shows its size, shape and measurements
examples: The original plans of the building have not survived.

senses.text: a diagram that shows how something will be arranged

senses.text: a large round object in space that moves around a star (such as the sun) and receives light from it

senses.text: used to mean ‘the world’, especially when talking about the environment
examples: the battle to save the planet

senses.text: a flat, usually round, dish that you put food on

senses.text: the amount of food that you can put on a plate

senses.text: a flat surface raised above the level of the ground or floor, used by public speakers or performers so that the audience can see them

senses.text: a raised level surface, for example one that equipment stands on or is operated from

senses.text: fun, attractive, or giving pleasure
examples: a pleasant evening/atmosphere/walk
a pleasant aroma/smell/scent

music that is pleasant to the ear

senses.text: friendly and polite

senses.text: feeling happy about something

senses.text: happy or willing to do something

senses.text: a sudden decrease in an amount or the value of something
an act of jumping or diving into water; a quick swim

She didn't feel safe on her own.
Will the car be safe parked in the road?
They aimed to make the country safe from terrorist attacks.

senses.text: not likely to lead to any physical harm or danger
examples: a safe and effective remedy for coughs and colds

Acupuncture is safe for most people.
The street is not safe for children to play in.
It is one of the safest cars in the world.

senses.text: not harmed, damaged, lost, etc.
examples: We were glad she let us know she was safe.
The missing child was found safe and well.
They turned up safe and sound.
A reward was offered for the animal's safe return.
Take care of yourselves and stay safe.

senses.text: where somebody/something is not likely to be in danger or to be lost
examples: We all want to live in safer cities.
Keep your passport in a safe place.
We watched the explosion from a safe distance.
It was a relatively safe place compared with other countries.

senses.text: not involving much or any risk; not likely to be wrong or to upset somebody
examples: Electricity shares are still a safe investment.
As I peruse the menu the only safe bet is the grilled chicken.
The source of Harry's wealth was not a safe subject for public discussion.

senses.text: the state of being safe and protected from danger or harm
examples: a place where children can play in safety

measures to ensure patient safety
His behaviour endangered the safety of the public.

senses.text: the state of not being dangerous
examples: I'm worried about the safety of the treatment.
a local campaign to improve road safety
People worry about food safety (= food that could be harmful to health).
breaches of fire safety regulations
safety standards/measures/precautions

senses.text: a place where you are safe
examples: They reached safety seconds before the building was engulfed in flames.
I managed to swim to safety.

senses.text: a sheet of strong cloth which the wind blows against to make a boat or ship travel through the water
examples: As the boat moved down the river the wind began to fill the sails.
a ship under sail (= using sails)
in the days of sail (= when ships all used sails)

senses.text: a trip in a boat or ship
examples: We went for a sail.

senses.text: to travel on water using sails or an engine

one of the first people to sail around the world

senses.text: to control or travel on a boat with a sail, especially as a sport

the first woman to sail solo around the world

senses.text: to begin a journey on water
examples: We sail at 2 p.m. tomorrow.

senses.text: a person who works on a ship as a member of the crew

senses.text: a person who sails a boat

senses.text: a mixture of raw vegetables such as lettuce, tomato and cucumber, usually served with other food as part of a meal

a side salad (= a small bowl of salad served with the main course of a meal)

senses.text: meat, fish, cheese, etc. served with salad

senses.text: raw or cooked vegetables, etc. that are cut into small pieces, often mixed with mayonnaise and served cold with other food
examples: potato salad
a pasta salad

senses.text: exactly the one or ones referred to or mentioned; not different

senses.text: the same thing or things

senses.text: having the same number, colour, size, quality, etc.
examples: There are several brands and they're not all the same.

senses.text: an official order that limits trade, contact, etc. with a particular country, in order to make it do something, such as obeying international law

senses.text: a course of action that can be used, if necessary, to make people obey a law or behave in a particular way

senses.text: a substance that consists of very small fine grains of rock. Sand is found on beaches, in deserts, etc.
examples: a grain of sand
coarse/fine sand

The children were playing in the sand (= for example, in a sandpit).

senses.text: a large area of sand on a beach or in the desert
examples: We went for a walk along the sand.
miles of golden sands
the burning desert sands

senses.text: the state of having a healthy mind
examples: His behaviour was so strange that I began to question his sanity.

senses.text: the state of being sensible and reasonable

senses.text: to make somebody pleased by doing or giving them what they want

senses.text: to provide what is wanted, needed or asked for
examples: The food wasn't enough to satisfy his hunger.

senses.text: to make somebody certain something is true or has been done
examples: Her explanation did not satisfy the teacher.

senses.text: a situation in which a lot of people are anxious or frightened about something
examples: a health scare
recent scares about pesticides in food
a scare story (= a news report that spreads more anxiety or fear about something than is necessary)

scare tactics (= ways of persuading people to do something by frightening them)

senses.text: a sudden feeling of fear
examples: You gave me a scare!

senses.text: to frighten somebody
examples: You scared me.

senses.text: to become frightened

senses.text: a place where children go to be educated
examples: My brother and I went to the same school.

senses.text: the process of learning in a school; the time during your life when you go to a school
examples: to start/leave school

senses.text: a place where people go to learn a particular subject or skill
examples: a drama/language/riding school

senses.text: knowledge about the structure and behaviour of the natural and physical world, based on facts that you can prove, for example by experiments

the advance of modern science
the laws of science

senses.text: the study of science

senses.text: a particular branch of science
examples: medical/environmental science

senses.text: to rub your skin with your nails, usually because it is itching

senses.text: to cut or damage your skin slightly with something sharp

senses.text: to damage the surface of something, especially by accident, by making thin shallow marks on it

senses.text: to give a loud, high shout, because you are hurt, frightened, excited, etc.

senses.text: to shout something in a loud, high voice because of fear, anger, etc.

senses.text: any of the four main periods of the year: spring, summer, autumn and winter

senses.text: a place where you can sit, for example a chair

senses.text: to give something to somebody in exchange for money

senses.text: to offer something for people to buy

senses.text: a system that provides something that the public needs, organized by the government or a private company

senses.text: the programmes broadcast on television
examples: We don't do much in the evenings except watch television.

senses.text: the system, process or business of broadcasting television programmes
examples: satellite/terrestrial/digital/network television
These plays were originally broadcast on live television.
I'd like to work in television (= for a television company).
a television show/series/programme/broadcast
a television drama/documentary/interview/commercial

senses.text: to attract somebody or make somebody want to do or have something, even if they know it is wrong
examples: I was tempted by the dessert menu.
Don't tempt thieves by leaving valuables clearly visible.
He was tempted into a life of crime.
I was tempted to take the day off.

senses.text: to persuade or try to persuade somebody to do something that you want them to do, for example by offering them something
examples: How can we tempt young people into engineering?
Nothing would tempt me to live here.

senses.text: if somebody/something has a particular tendency, they are likely to behave or act in a particular way
examples: to display artistic tendencies
I have a tendency to talk too much when I'm nervous.
This material has a tendency to shrink when washed.
There is a tendency for this disease to run in families.
She has a strong natural tendency towards caution.

senses.text: a new custom that is starting to develop
examples: There's a growing tendency for women to marry later.
Industry showed a tendency towards increasingly centralized administration.

senses.text: a building or set of buildings at an airport where passengers arrive and leave
examples: A second terminal was opened last year.
Your flight leaves from Terminal 3.

senses.text: a place, building or set of buildings where journeys by train, bus or boat begin or end
examples: a railway/bus/ferry terminal

senses.text: a medical examination to discover what is wrong with you or to check the condition of your health
examples: The hospital is doing some tests.
We'll need to perform a series of tests.
screening tests for cancer
Three athletes were sent home after failing drugs tests.
The pregnancy test was positive.

senses.text: an experiment to discover whether or how well something works, or to find out more information about it
examples: the results of laboratory tests
to conduct a nuclear test
Tests have shown high levels of pollutants in the water.
I'll run a diagnostic test to determine why the server keeps crashing.

senses.text: the way a surface, substance or piece of cloth feels when you touch it, for example how rough, smooth, hard or soft it is
examples: the soft texture of velvet
She uses a variety of different colours and textures in her wall hangings.
The method used will vary with the soil texture and climate.

senses.text: the way food or drink tastes or feels in your mouth, for example whether it is rough, smooth, light, heavy, etc.
examples: The two cheeses were very different in both taste and texture.

senses.text: a building or an outdoor area where plays and similar types of entertainment are performed
examples: Broadway theatres
an open-air theatre
How often do you go to the theatre?
She left the theatre a few minutes after the curtain fell.

senses.text: a building in which films are shown
examples: They arrived at the movie theater a few minutes later.
I urge you to go see this film when it comes to a theater near you.

senses.text: the study of religion and beliefs
examples: a degree in theology
a theology student
the rarefied world of academic theology

senses.text: a set of religious beliefs
examples: the theologies of the East

senses.text: connected with the ideas and principles on which a particular subject is based, rather than with practice and experiment
examples: a theoretical approach
theoretical physics
The first year provides students with a sound theoretical basis for later study.

senses.text: that could possibly exist, happen or be true, although this is unlikely
examples: It's a theoretical possibility.

senses.text: a formal set of ideas that is intended to explain why something happens or exists
examples: the theory of evolution/relativity
scientific/economic theory
The theories were tested on a sample of the population.
He developed a new theory about the cause of stomach ulcers.
theories on human behaviour

senses.text: the principles on which a particular subject is based
examples: the theory and practice of language teaching
This is your chance to put theory into practice .
literary theory

senses.text: an opinion or idea that somebody believes is true but that is not proved
examples: Theories abound (= people have lots of different ideas) about what happened.
Here's my theory on how the story's going to end.
I don't subscribe to the theory that all Hollywood audiences want a happy ending.
Police are working on the theory that the murderer was known to the family.

senses.text: a specialist who treats a particular type of illness or problem, or who uses a particular type of treatment
examples: a speech therapist
a beauty therapist

senses.text: a person who is trained to treat somebody who has problems with their mental health by talking with them
the treatment of a physical problem or an illness
examples: He is receiving therapy for cancer.
Most leukaemia patients undergo some sort of drug therapy (= treatment using drugs).
alternative/complementary therapies (= treatments that do not use traditional drugs)
new cancer therapies
They recommend exercise therapy for patients with chronic back pain.

senses.text: the treatment of problems with somebody's mental health by talking with them
examples: She's in therapy.
I went into therapy because my doctor suggested it.
the use of hypnosis as a form of therapy
a therapy group/session

senses.text: growing closely together in large amounts or numbers
examples: thick dark hair
This breed of cattle has a very thick coat.
His eyebrows were thick and bushy.
a thick forest

senses.text: not flowing very easily
examples: thick soup
The effect will be ruined if the paint is too thick.
I was buried up to my waist in thick mud, unable to move.

senses.text: difficult to see through; difficult to breathe in
examples: The plane crashed in thick fog.
Thick black smoke rose over the city.
Thick cloud covered the sky.
The air had grown thick and smoky.
The air was thick with dust.

senses.text: to send something from your hand through the air by moving your hand or arm quickly
examples: Practise throwing and catching.
They had a competition to see who could throw the furthest.
Who threw that rock?
Stop throwing stones at the window!
She threw the ball up and caught it again.

senses.text: to put something in a particular place quickly and carelessly
examples: Just throw your bag down over there.
He threw the keys casually down on the table.
A blanket was thrown over the bed.
I took some folders and started throwing stuff in them.

senses.text: to move something/somebody suddenly and with force
examples: The boat was thrown onto the rocks.
The sea throws up all sorts of debris on the beach.
They were thrown to the floor by the impact.
He was arrested and thrown in jail (= sent to prison).
He threatened to throw her in the river if she screamed.

senses.text: to move your body or part of it quickly or suddenly
examples: He threw back his head and roared with laughter.
I ran up and threw my arms around him.
Jenny threw herself onto the bed.
I just wanted to throw myself into his arms and cry.

senses.text: arranged neatly and with everything in order
examples: a tidy desk
She keeps her flat very tidy.
I like everything to be neat and tidy.
The room was clean and tidy.
The place has never looked so tidy.

senses.text: keeping things neat and in order
examples: I'm a tidy person.
He is obsessively tidy.
tidy habits

senses.text: to attach or hold two or more things together using string, rope, etc.; to fasten somebody/something with string, rope, etc.
examples: She tied the newspapers in a bundle.
He had to tie her hands together.
They tied him to a chair with cable.
I tie back my hair when I'm cooking.
The box was tied with plastic string.

senses.text: to fasten something to or around something else
examples: She tied a label on to the suitcase.
Anything which might be blown over was tied down or moved inside.

senses.text: to make a knot in a piece of string, rope, etc.
examples: to tie a ribbon
Can you help me tie my tie?
Tie the cords neatly.
Can you tie a bow tie?
He tied his shoes (= the laces on his shoes) with a double knot.

senses.text: held or fixed in position securely; difficult to move, open or separate
examples: He kept a tight grip on her arm.
She twisted her hair into a tight knot.
The screw was so tight that it wouldn't move.
Dave's arms were tight around her waist.

senses.text: fitting closely to your body and sometimes uncomfortable
examples: She was wearing a tight pair of jeans.
These shoes are much too tight.
The new sweater was a tight fit.
The dress is a little tight across the chest.

senses.text: difficult to manage with because there is not enough
examples: We have a very tight budget.
The president has a tight schedule today.
The deadline for finishing the project is too tight (= too soon).
I think we'd better leave—time's getting very tight.
As always, money is tight.

senses.text: very strict and hard to resist, avoid, etc.
examples: to keep tight control over something
Dozens of streets are being closed and security is tight.
We need tighter security at the airport.
Are the controls tight enough?
tight restrictions/regulations

senses.text: a machine used in shops, restaurants, etc. for keeping money in, and that shows and records the amount of money received for each thing that is sold
the place where you pay for goods in a large shop
examples: Please pay at the till.
a long queue at the till
The supermarket didn't have enough people working on the tills.

senses.text: the drawer where the money is put in a cash register
the act of choosing when something happens; a particular point or period of time when something happens or is planned
examples: The timing of the decision was a complete surprise.
Please check your flight timings carefully.

senses.text: the skill of doing something at exactly the right time
examples: an actor with a great sense of comic timing
Your timing is perfect. I was just about to call you.

senses.text: a metal container in which food is sold; the contents of one of these containers
examples: Open the tin and drain the tuna.
a tin of beans/soup
Next, add two tins of tomatoes.
a sardine tin

senses.text: a metal container with a lid (= cover), in the shape of a cylinder, in which paint, glue, etc. is sold and stored; the contents of one of these containers
examples: a tin of varnish
The bedroom needed three tins of paint (= in order to paint it).
a paint tin

senses.text: a metal container used for cooking food in
examples: a cake/loaf tin

senses.text: a word in front of a person’s name to show their rank or profession, whether or not they are married, etc.
examples: The present duke inherited the title from his father.
Give your name and title (= Mr, Miss, Ms, Dr, etc.).

senses.text: a name that describes a job
examples: The official title of the job is ‘Administrative Assistant’.
What is your name and job title?
Bessie Smith earned the title ‘Empress of the Blues’.
In 2017, Brooks assumed the title of chairman.

senses.text: one of the five small parts that stick out from the foot; any similar part on the foot of an animal or bird
examples: the big/little toe (= the largest/smallest toe)
I stubbed my toe on the step.
Can you touch your toes (= by bending over while keeping your legs straight)?
She wiggled her fingers and toes thoughtfully.
She stood on her toes and kissed him.

senses.text: the part of a sock, shoe, etc. that covers the toes
examples: He kicked the earth with the toe of his boot.

senses.text: the act of causing somebody severe pain in order to punish them or make them say or do something
examples: Many of the refugees have suffered torture.
the use of torture
terrible instruments of torture
His confessions were made under torture.
I heard stories of gruesome tortures in prisons.

senses.text: mental or physical pain; something that causes this
examples: The interview was sheer torture from start to finish.

senses.text: to throw something lightly or carelessly
examples: I tossed the book aside and got up.
He tossed the ball to Anna.
He tossed Anna the ball.

senses.text: to throw a coin in the air in order to decide something, especially by guessing which side is facing upwards when it lands
examples: Let's toss a coin.
There's only one ticket left—I'll toss you for it.
We tossed up to see who went first.
He had to toss up between (= decide between) paying the rent or buying food.

senses.text: being part of the beliefs, customs or way of life of a particular group of people, that have not changed for a long time
examples: traditional dress/music/art/culture/dance
It's traditional in America to eat turkey on Thanksgiving Day.
traditional values/practices/beliefs
Acupuncture has long been a part of traditional Chinese medicine.
Janet makes cheese by traditional methods.

senses.text: following older methods and ideas rather than modern or different ones
examples: a traditional method/way of doing something
traditional methods of teaching
a traditional approach/view
Their marriage is very traditional.
the traditional female role of homemaker

senses.text: to move from one place to another; to move something/somebody from one place to another
examples: The film studio is transferring to Hollywood.
If I spend a semester in Madrid, will my credits transfer?
The system only allows air to go one way, preventing air from transferring from one tire to another.
How can I transfer money from my bank account to his?
The patient was transferred to another hospital.

senses.text: to move from one job, school, situation, etc. to another; to arrange for somebody to move
examples: Children usually transfer to secondary school at 11 or 12.
This is her first season in the Ohio State team after transferring from Tennessee.
Students can transfer from a community college to a university.
He transferred to UCLA after his freshman year.
Ten employees are being transferred from the sales department.

senses.text: to change to a different vehicle during a journey; to arrange for somebody to change to a different vehicle during a journey
examples: It was a relief, after transferring from ship to ship, to just sit and relax.
I transferred at Bahrain for a flight to Singapore.
Passengers are transferred from the airport to the hotel by taxi.

senses.text: to copy information, music, an idea, etc. from one method of recording or presenting it to another; to be recorded or presented in a different way
examples: You can transfer data to a memory stick in a few seconds.
Data is easily transferred electronically.
You can transfer songs from your computer to a phone.
The novel does not transfer well to the movies.
An audio file should transfer in a matter of minutes.

senses.text: if you transfer a feeling, a disease, or power, etc. from one person to another, the second person has it, often instead of the first
examples: Joe had already transferred his affections from Lisa to Cleo.
I decided to transfer my loyalty to my local team.
This disease is sometimes transferred from mother to baby (= so that the baby has it as well as the mother).

senses.text: to change the form of something; to change in form
examples: The photochemical reactions transform the light into electrical impulses.
Materials are transformed from their original state into other forms.
I am convinced that the electric power industry is transforming.
The way we work, play and communicate every day has completely transformed from a decade ago.

senses.text: to completely change the appearance or character of something/somebody, especially so that it is better; to completely change in appearance or character
examples: A new colour scheme will transform your bedroom.
It was an event that would transform my life.
During the performance the artist transforms herself with the aid of make-up and a wig.
London's Docklands have been radically transformed over the last 20 years.
He seems to have been miraculously transformed into a first-class player.

senses.text: to express the meaning of speech or writing in a different language
examples: He translated the letter into English.
Her books have been translated into 24 languages.
Can you help me translate this legal jargon into plain English?
This chapter was translated from the French by Oliver Breen.
His works have been translated from French into countless languages.

senses.text: to be changed from one language to another
examples: Most poetry does not translate well.
The Welsh name translates as ‘Land's End’.
It's a Spanish word that roughly translates as 'unease'.
‘Tiramisu’ literally translates as ‘pull-me-up’.

senses.text: the process of changing something that is written or spoken into another language
examples: an error in translation
He specializes in translation from Danish into English.
The request must have lost something in the translation from the French.
Several books are already in translation into other languages.
The poems do not survive the translation into English.

senses.text: a text or word that has been changed from one language into another
examples: The usual translation of ‘glasnost’ is ‘openness’.
a copy of Dryden’s translation of the Aeneid
A full English translation of this speech is widely available on the internet.
a rough translation (= not translating everything exactly)
a literal translation (= following the original words exactly)

senses.text: a system for carrying people or goods from one place to another using vehicles, roads, etc.
examples: air/freight/road transport
the government’s transport policy
The region has good transport links.
We need a reliable transport network.
Modern transport systems have been a severe threat to the environment.

senses.text: a vehicle or method of travel
examples: Applicants must have their own transport.
Transport to and from the airport is included in the price.
His bike is his only means of transport.
The train is the preferred mode of transport for commuters working in Dublin.
Hitchhiking is a common form of transport.

senses.text: a mental condition caused by severe shock, stress or fear, especially when the harmful effects last for a long time
examples: the effects of trauma and stress on the body
The children showed no signs of trauma after their ordeal.
patients who have histories of childhood trauma

senses.text: a very frightening or upsetting experience
examples: She felt exhausted after the traumas of recent weeks.

senses.text: to go from one place to another, especially over a long distance
examples: I go to bed early if I'm travelling the next day.
to travel around the world
I love travelling by train.
We always travel first class.
I travel abroad a lot.

senses.text: to go or move at a particular speed, in a particular direction, or a particular distance
examples: to travel at 50 miles an hour
Messages travel along the spine from the nerve endings to the brain.
News travels fast these days.
He never travelled far from the home he shared with his mother.
The wide streets allow cars to travel at high speeds.

senses.text: to put your foot down while you are stepping or walking
examples: Ouch! You trod on my toe!
Careful you don't tread in that puddle.

senses.text: to move, push or press something with your feet
examples: Don't tread ash into the carpet!
The wine is still made by treading grapes in the traditional way.
She planted the seeds and trod the earth down.

senses.text: to walk somewhere
examples: Few people had trod this path before.
He was treading quietly and cautiously.
